And we see that globally, around the world, there's better job prospects, there's better education and health care sectors. And so on the one hand, there is frustration among domestic, foreign, excuse me, domestic South Africans, because they see increasing competition and pressure because of what they see as more and more people coming in and trying to compete for the few jobs that they see that they have. And we should point out, unemployment here has been north of 30% since Covid. And so, it is an economic story on the one hand, but I think you also have to take a look at what we've been seeing globally with this immigration story. It's not just centered here in South Africa. We know, of course, in the US, President Trump has made mass deportations in his second term, sort of a pillar of the second term. We know in Germany, the far right AfD party has also focused in on this as well, as it's been a part of the rhetoric in the UK as well. And so, it is a bit complicated when when you dig beneath the surface. But I think really when, when we get to the heart of it here, it seems to be an economic story of frustration. But you can't, separate from the fact that a lot of the economic story ends up being entangled with a lot of the workers who are working domestically. Yeah. All right, Jen, thank you for that update. Jennifer herself, our chief Africa correspondent, on top of that story for us in Johannesburg. Now onto some other stories we're following. President Donald Trump will temporarily lift duties on phosphate fertilizer from Morocco to aid a struggling farm economy and address consumer fears over food inflation. The suspension will last for eight months or until the emergency is terminated, and is intended to improve fertilizer availability and help reduce input costs for farmers and cobalt. Miners in the Democratic Republic of Congo will be required to surrender unused export quotas for the first half of the year to a government regulator. The world's dominant supplier of the battery metal has imposed strict limits on shipments since early last year, replacing an all out ban with quotas in October and coming up, Andy Burnham vows to enforce the biggest rebalancing of power in the UK that it has seen. If he succeeds in becoming the next prime minister. The key takeaways from his speech in Manchester next. This is Bloomberg. Andy Burnham has pledged to radically evolve fiscal powers away from Westminster if he succeeds, to his bid to replace Keir Starmer as prime minister. In a speech in Manchester, the man who served as the region's mayor until last week said he would swap trickle down economics for, quote, the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen in. All parts of the UK. Should now be given the chance to develop their own version of the same, focusing on the things that matter most to them. Good growth in every British postcode. What a sea change that would be in the way UK government thinks about our own country. For more on that speech, let's bring in Bloomberg's Lizzie Burton. Lizzie, good to have you on. What are the key takeaways that you have seen from the Burnham speech? Well, I'll tell you a bit of it. If Keir Starmer thrived anywhere, it was on the global stage. But Andy Burnham is being much more local in his focus. And this is a speech to explain what Manchester is a means to him. As you say, he's a former mayor of Greater Manchester. He had a huge emphasis on devolution, moving power away from Westminster into the regions. He talked about the biggest housebuilding program in the postwar period. He talked about easing the cost of living for ordinary Britons. But frankly, these are all pledges that we've heard before to level up Britain and to ease the cost of living, to build more homes. They're all goals that Brits have been promised before by previous prime ministers. So we're waiting for more detail on how he'll actually make it happen. It was vibes, heavy, detail, light and frankly, it was quite frustrating because this was billed as the first look at his economic policy. But he said that we're going to have to wait several weeks until the conclusion of the Labor Party leadership contest, first, to find out who that chancellor is going to be. And Lizzie, the risks to investors, what are they at the moment? Well, Bloomberg Economics has done some research into this, and they say that the main risk is that while Burnham's at the moment trying to focus on a ten year plan, ten year goals because of the looming need to call a general election by the latest 2029, he's going to get tempted into short term giveaways and that actually, that means he has to juice the economy over the next few years. And we've seen so many prime ministers devoured in short periods of time. What's to say that Burnham won't be, weak to that pressure as well? But they did flag that his focus on a ten year window could suggest that he also wants to change the fiscal rules, moves the goalpost a bit so that they're achieved over a ten year period over the longer term, rather than the medium term. That could worry markets. On the positive side, there are economists saying that perhaps this devolution agenda makes sense because you need to even out regional inequality. And the UK is the most fiscally centralized in the G7. All right, Lizzie, we're going to have to leave it there. Lizzie Burton in London, anchoring daybreak in just a couple of minutes. Now that's it for Horizon's Middle East and Africa. I'm available only in Dubai. Stay around with us for Daybreak Europe. This is Bloomberg.
